meta.title: OKF Is a Projection, Not the Whole System
meta.audience: technical marketers and content operators adopting agent-readable content

uniqueInsight: Most teams treat an Open Knowledge Format bundle as the canonical artifact; it is better understood as one projection of a single canonical model, so the real leverage is owning the model and generating many discovery surfaces from it.

internalLinks.reason: OKF is one of several views materialized from the same graph.
Explains why each projected surface must self-bootstrap for an agent.

citations.why: Primary description of the format this article reframes.
Grounds the determinism claim for projected, verifiable surfaces.

draft:
## OKF is a projection, not the whole system

Teams adopting agent-readable content often ask "should we publish OKF?" as if the bundle were the product. It is not. OKF is one projection of a canonical knowledge model. Own the model, and OKF, HTML, JSON-LD, and a markdown twin all fall out of it deterministically.

The practical payoff: you stop hand-maintaining four surfaces that drift apart, and you start generating them from one source whose every view can be recomputed and checked.

### What to do

1. Model the concept once, with its entities and relationships.
2. Project the surfaces an agent might fetch first.
3. Bind each projection to the model with a digest so a consumer can verify it.

faq.question: Is OKF a replacement for a knowledge graph?
faq.answer: No. OKF is a portable projection of a graph; the graph (or canonical model) remains the source of truth.

faq.question: Why generate multiple surfaces instead of just publishing OKF?
faq.answer: Different agents fetch different surfaces first; projecting them all from one model keeps them consistent and verifiable.
